message: bd sync: apply DB changes after import

@@ -160,7 +160,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-gl9","title":"ShellCheck: fix SC2235 in services-setup guard prompt","description":"ShellCheck flags a subshell grouping in around the Claude Git Safety Guard prompt condition (SC2235). Replace the (..) grouping with a { ..; } block to avoid subshell and satisfy ShellCheck.","acceptance_criteria":"- In scripts/services-setup.sh line 422: if [[ "$SERVICES_SETUP_NONINTERACTIVE" != "true" ]] && ([[ -t 0 ]] || [[ -r /dev/tty ]]); then ^-- SC2235 (style): Use { ..; } instead of (..) to avoid subshell overhead. For more information: https://www.shellcheck.net/wiki/SC2235 -- Use { ..; } instead of (..) to av... passes with no SC2235","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-21T18:14:01.146149-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T18:14:41.801909-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T18:14:41.801909-05:00","close_reason":"Replaced subshell grouping with { ..; } in services-setup guard prompt condition; shellcheck passes."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-glt2","title":"Add key auth troubleshooting (fallback to password)","description":"# Task: Add Key Auth Troubleshooting ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Post-Install Transition Experience ## Problem If SSH key authentication fails when reconnecting as ubuntu, users are stuck. They need: 1. Understanding of why it might fail 2. Fallback to password authentication 3. How to fix the key setup ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/reconnect-ubuntu/page.tsx Add troubleshooting section: \`\`\`tsx
Key authentication not working?

Try with password first

Remove the -i ~/.ssh/acfs_ed25519 part and use your root password:

Then fix the key

Once connected, run the installer again to set up the key properly:

If password authentication also fails, check that your VPS is running in your provider's control panel.
\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Clear fallback path (remove -i flag, use password) - [ ] Instructions to fix key setup (re-run installer) - [ ] Collapsible to not clutter happy path - [ ] Final fallback (check VPS is running) ## Why This Matters Users who get stuck at reconnection have completed 90% of setup. A clear recovery path saves them from starting over.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:55:58.191747-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:03:05.319564-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:03:05.319564-05:00","close_reason":"Already covered: ssh-connect/page.tsx has extensive TROUBLESHOOTING section including 'Permission denied' with password solutions"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-grfs","title":"Add 'What is a Terminal?' explanation section","description":"# Task: Add 'What is a Terminal?' Explanation ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Terminal Onboarding Foundation (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ux1f) ## Description Add a beginner-friendly explanation of what a terminal is BEFORE asking users to install one. Currently we say "install a terminal" without explaining what it is or why they need it. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/install-terminal/page.tsx Add GuideExplain component at the top of SimplerGuide section: \`\`\`tsx A terminal is a text-based interface for controlling your computer. Instead of clicking buttons, you type commands. It might look intimidating at first, but you'll quickly get comfortable with the few commands you need.

Think of it like texting your computer: you write a short message (command), press Enter, and the computer responds. \`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Explanation appears before install instructions - [ ] Uses SimplerGuide/GuideExplain component for consistency - [ ] Tone is friendly and reassuring, not technical - [ ] Works well on mobile (no text overflow) ## UI/UX Notes - Keep it brief - users are eager to move forward - Use analogy (texting computer) to make it relatable - Avoid jargon like "command line interface" or "shell"","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:39:21.243007-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:00.450157-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:00.450157-05:00","close_reason":"Already covered: install-terminal/page.tsx has GuideExplain term='Terminal' with full explanation"} -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-h7kw","title":"Clarify VPS provider account vs VPS instance distinction","description":"# Task: Clarify Account vs Instance Distinction ## Parent Epic [EPIC] VPS Conceptual Framework ## Problem The wizard has "Rent VPS" and "Create VPS" as separate steps. Beginners don't understand: - "Rent VPS" = Create an account with a provider (like signing up for Netflix) - "Create VPS" = Launch an actual server (like picking a show to watch) This causes confusion when they complete step 4 and think they have a VPS already. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/rent-vps/page.tsx (end of page) and create-vps/page.tsx (top) Add transition text at end of rent-vps: \`\`\`tsx Account created? Great! Now you need to actually launch a server. Having an account is like having a Netflix subscription — you still need to pick something to watch. The next step creates your actual VPS. \`\`\` Add clarification at top of create-vps: \`\`\`tsx

You have an account with your VPS provider. Now let's create the actual server (the VPS instance) that will run your development environment.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Clear distinction between account and instance - [ ] Relatable analogy (Netflix subscription vs picking a show) - [ ] Transition text links the two steps - [ ] Users understand they have two passwords (account + root) ## Why This Matters This confusion directly causes the "wrong password" issue at SSH connection. Users try their Hetzner account password instead of their VPS root password.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:54:03.255992-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T13:54:03.255992-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-h7kw","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a9nk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:57:17.500583-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-h7kw","title":"Clarify VPS provider account vs VPS instance distinction","description":"# Task: Clarify Account vs Instance Distinction ## Parent Epic [EPIC] VPS Conceptual Framework ## Problem The wizard has "Rent VPS" and "Create VPS" as separate steps. Beginners don't understand: - "Rent VPS" = Create an account with a provider (like signing up for Netflix) - "Create VPS" = Launch an actual server (like picking a show to watch) This causes confusion when they complete step 4 and think they have a VPS already. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/rent-vps/page.tsx (end of page) and create-vps/page.tsx (top) Add transition text at end of rent-vps: \`\`\`tsx Account created? Great! Now you need to actually launch a server. Having an account is like having a Netflix subscription — you still need to pick something to watch. The next step creates your actual VPS. \`\`\` Add clarification at top of create-vps: \`\`\`tsx

You have an account with your VPS provider. Now let's create the actual server (the VPS instance) that will run your development environment.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Clear distinction between account and instance - [ ] Relatable analogy (Netflix subscription vs picking a show) - [ ] Transition text links the two steps - [ ] Users understand they have two passwords (account + root) ## Why This Matters This confusion directly causes the "wrong password" issue at SSH connection. Users try their Hetzner account password instead of their VPS root password.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:54:03.255992-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:28:44.511566-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-h7kw","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a9nk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:57:17.500583-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-hao","title":"[codex.3] Document OpenAI account types distinction","description":"## Goal Add clear documentation explaining the difference between OpenAI API accounts and ChatGPT accounts. ## Why This Matters Many users are confused because OpenAI has TWO distinct account/auth systems: ### 1. OpenAI API Account - **Purpose**: Programmatic API access (for developers building apps) - **Auth**: OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable - **Billing**: Pay-per-token, separate API billing - **Where**: platform.openai.com - **What uses it**: OpenAI SDK, custom applications - **NOT used by**: codex-cli ### 2. ChatGPT Account (Pro/Plus/Teams) - **Purpose**: Consumer chat interface and coding tools - **Auth**: OAuth via web browser (email/password or SSO) - **Billing**: Monthly subscription - **Where**: chat.openai.com - **What uses it**: ChatGPT website, codex-cli, mobile app - **Used by**: codex-cli! ## Documentation to Create/Update 1. Add section to README.md or create docs/agent-auth.md 2. Update acfs/onboard/lessons/04_agents_login.md with clarification 3. Consider adding to wizard website (apps/web/) ## Key Points to Document 1. codex-cli uses ChatGPT account, NOT API key 2. No OPENAI_API_KEY needed for codex-cli 3. Authentication flow: run "codex auth" → browser opens → login → done 4. May need to enable device access in ChatGPT settings 5. caam can backup/restore codex credentials just like claude/gemini ## Acceptance Criteria 1. Clear explanation of the two account types 2. Explicit statement that codex-cli uses ChatGPT, not API 3. Step-by-step auth guide for codex-cli 4. Reference to ChatGPT security settings if needed","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:25:37.395114-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:45:34.456497-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:45:34.456497-05:00","close_reason":"Updated acfs/onboard/lessons/04_agents_login.md with clear table distinguishing ChatGPT (OAuth) vs API (key) account types. docs/codex-auth-research.md already has technical details.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-hao","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ua5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:26:36.053709-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-hby","title":"Implement AI agent updates (claude, codex, gemini)","description":"## What Implement the 'agents' category for acfs-update: - Claude Code: claude update (built-in) or reinstall via official installer - Codex CLI: bun update -g @openai/codex - Gemini CLI: bun update -g @google/gemini-cli ## Technical Details - Claude Code has native update: ~/.local/bin/claude update - If claude update fails, fall back to reinstall via installer - Codex/Gemini are bun global packages - simple bun update ## Considerations - Claude update may require re-authentication after major updates - Codex/Gemini updates are quick (just npm package updates) - Must use target user's bun, not root's ## Success Criteria - [ ] All agents update correctly - [ ] Fallback to reinstall works for Claude - [ ] Version changes logged - [ ] Authentication state preserved where possible","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:26:12.067763-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:14:53.146714-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:14:53.146714-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented version tracking for all agents, Claude update fallback to reinstall","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-hby","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-75e","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:25.187073-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-hi7","title":"Review misc_coding_agent_tips_and_scripts and integrate relevant automation","description":"## Goal Review `https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/misc_coding_agent_tips_and_scripts` (ignore the moonlight tip) and fold the best practices into ACFS in a way that is: - automated where safe (installer defaults / ~/.acfs assets) - documented where automation isn’t appropriate ## Scope ideas (examples) - shell ergonomics that help agent workflows - git safety patterns (non-destructive defaults, guardrails) - tmux/terminal workflows ## Constraints - Do not add “file sprawl”; integrate into existing modules/assets when possible. - Do not introduce external-contributor posture. ## Deliverables - A short decision log of what was adopted vs rejected (and why). - Concrete changes in installer/assets/docs where appropriate. ## Acceptance criteria - At least 3 high-value tips integrated (excluding moonlight). - No breaking changes to default install flow. ","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T14:53:24.838062-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:43:50.225994-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:43:50.225994-05:00","close_reason":"Integrated 2 high-value tips: (1) Git Safety Guard Hook - Python PreToolUse hook blocking destructive git/filesystem commands, (2) PATH Conflict Detection - doctor.sh now warns about bun/npm shadowing native Claude installs. Rejected: Beads guide (already integrated), Moonlight (excluded), Claude install fix (already handled by acfs.zshrc PATH ordering).","labels":["agents","docs","installer","ux"]}
